content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J S1rce1 • Suire 620 • Sacramento. CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax. (916) 322-0886 
Mr. Dan Miller 
County of Marin Elections Department 
P. 0. Box E 
San Rafael, CA 94913 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-12-137 
Dear Mr. Miller: 
October 22, 2012 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because you are asking a general question rather than a 
question about a specific transaction, we are treating your request as one for informal 
• 
1 
assistance.-
QUESTION 
If Marin County desires to eliminate paper copies and the original signature requirement 
on electronically filed campaign statements, does AB 2452 require that the County mandate all 
of your local filers to file electronically? 
CONCLUSION 
No, under Section 84615 local jurisdictions are not mandated to require all candidates or 
committees to file campaign statements electronically to get the benefits of not requiring paper 
copies and original signatures on those statements filed electronically. 
Under the new legislation, local jurisdictions that use electronic campaign filing shall 
enact an ordinance with required findings approving the use of electronic filing. Section 84615 
permits a local jurisdiction to require candidates and committees that raise or spend $1,000 or 
more to file electronically, or to require electronic filing of specifically identified types of filings 
1 The Polilical Reform Acl is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 lhrough 91014. All sratutory 
references are lo the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulalory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assislance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
wriuen advice. (Section 83114; Regulation I 8329(c)(3).) 


File No. l-12-137 
Page No. 2 
or at a higher monetary threshold. However. Section 84615 does not prohibit Marin County 
from continuing its policy of allowing candidates and committees to file campaign reports either 
electronically or on paper. Further. we interpret Section 84615(j) to mean that for those filings 
that a local jurisdiction does receive electronically. no paper copy is required. 
FACTS 
It is your understanding that Assembly Bill 2452 allows local officers the ability to offer 
e-filing of campaign finance statements to its filers while eliminating the requirement to file by 
paper format. thus ending the need for original signatures. 
Currently your office, the County of Marin Elections Department. employs the use of an 
outside e-filing system, NetFile. You offer, but do not require, your filers the use of this system. 
After the statement is filed, the original signature is filed on paper. 
Roughly half your local filers submit statements in paper format. You have tried 
unsuccessfully to convince them to use e-file, but they have stated they are not comfortable using 
any e-file system. It is not only small campaigns. but some sitting county supervisors who object 
to using electronic filing in your jurisdiction. It appears to you that forcing filers to file 
electronically will "unduly burden" these types of filers. 
You post all filers• campaign statements online. The electronically filed statements are 
online and you scan and put online the campaign statements of those who file on paper. 
You would like to have a voluntary e-filing system that eliminates the need for paper and 
original signatures. If you desire to eliminate paper copies and the original signature 
requirement. does this bill require that you mandate all of your local filers to file electronically? 
ANALYSIS 
AB 2452 added Section 84615 to the Act, which states: 
.. A local government agency may require an elected officer, candidate, 
committee, or other person required to file statements. reports, or other documents 
required by Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 84100), except an elected 
officer. candidate. committee, or other person who receives contributions totaling 
less than one thousand dollars ($1,000), and makes expenditures totaling less than 
one thousand dollars ($1,000), in a calendar year. to file those statements, reports, 
or other documents online or electronically with a local filing officer. A local 
government agency that requires online or electronic filing pursuant to this 
section shall comply with all of the following: 
(a) The legislative body for the local government agency shall adopt an 
ordinance approving the use of online or electronic filing, which shall include a 
legislative finding that the online or electronic filing system will operate securely 


File No. 1-12-137 
Page No. 3 
and effectively and would not unduly burden filers. The ordinance adopted by the 
legislative body for the local government agency may, at the discretion of that 
legislative body, specify that the electronic or online filing requirements apply 
only to specifically identified types of filings or are triggered only by identified 
monetary thresholds. In any instance in which the original statement, report, or 
other document is required to be filed with the Secretary of State and a copy of 
that statement, report, or other document is required to be filed with the local 
government agency, the ordinance may permit, but shall not require, that the copy 
be filed online or electronically. 
(b) The online or electronic filing system shall only accept a filing in the 
standardized record format that is developed by the Secretary of State pursuant to 
paragraph (2) of subdivision (a) of Section 84602 and that is compatible with the 
Secretary of State's system for receiving an online or electronic filing. 
(c) The online or electronic filing system shall ensure the integrity of the data 
transmitted and shall include safeguards against efforts to tamper with, 
manipulate, alter, or subvert the data. 
( d) ( l) The local filing officer shall issue to a person who files a statement, 
report, or other document online or electronically an electronic confirmation that 
notifies the filer that the statement, report, or other document was received. The 
confirmation shall include the date and the time that the statement, report, or other 
document was received by the filing officer and the method by which the filer 
may view and print the data received by the filing officer. 
(2) A copy retained by the filer of a statement, report, or other document that 
was filed online or electronically and the confirmation issued pursuant to 
paragraph (1) that shows the filer timely filed the statement, report, or other 
document shall create a rebuttable presumption that the filer timely filed the 
statement, report, or other document. 
(e) The date of filing for a statement, report, or other document that is filed 
online or electronically shall be the day that it is received by the local filing 
officer. 
(f) The local filing officer shall make all the data filed available on the Internet 
in an easily understood format that provides the greatest public access. The data 
shall be made available free of charge and as soon as possible after receipt. The 
data made available on the Internet shall not contain the street name and building 
number of the persons or entity representatives listed on the electronically filed 
forms or any bank account number required to be disclosed by the filer. The local 
filing officer shall make a complete, unredacted copy of any statement, report, or 
other document filed pursuant to this section, including any street names, building 
numbers, and bank account numbers disclosed by the filer, available to any person 
upon request. 
(g) The online or electronic filing system shall include a procedure for filers to 
comply with the requirement that they sign statements and reports under penalty 
of perjury pursuant to Section 81004. 


File No. 1-12-137 
Page No. 4 
(h) The local government agency shall enable filers to complete and submit 
filings free of charge. 
(i) The local filing officer shall maintain, for a period of at least 10 years 
commencing from the date filed, a secured, official version of each online or 
electronic statement, report, or other document filed pursuant to this section, 
which shall serve as the official version of that record for purpose of audits and 
any other legal purpose. Data that has been maintained for at least 10 years may 
then be archived in a secure fonnat. 
(j) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, any statement, report, or other 
document filed online or electronically pursuant to this section shall not be 
required to be filed with the local filing officer in paper fonnat." 
The purpose of the legislation, as described by the author in the Senate Rules Committee 
analysis3 is as follows: 
"Many campaign statements required by the Political Refonn Act of 1974 filed 
by persons and committees at the state and local level are archived electronically 
and are available to the public on the Internet. Currently, persons and committees 
filing these campaign fonns electronically must still print up to two paper copies 
of the fonns already posted online and mail them to a local filing officer. These 
officers must then process and archive copious amounts of paper documents 
already available online. Not only is this requirement redundant, but operating 
costs are increased because agencies must pay pennanent and seasonal staff to 
process fonns and maintain paper archives. In San Francisco, it has become so 
unwieldy that the Ethics Commission was forced to rent additional office space 
and off-site storage space to accommodate the growing mass of paper files . .. 
AB 2452 would authorize local ethics agencies to require that all persons or 
committees filing campaign documents or reports must do so online or 
electronically. These changes would enonnously reduce paper waste and allow 
county clerks to focus more attention to helping filers comply with campaign 
disclosure requirements instead of managing, in many cases, both electronic and 
paper-based filing systems containing the exact same data." 
''Many local clerks and elections officials have implemented their own online or 
electronic disclosure systems. In some cases, the local jurisdictions require 
candidates and committees to file disclosure reports online or electronically 
pursuant to a local campaign ordinance. Even in these circumstances, however, 
the PRA still generally requires paper copies of those reports to be filed with the 
local officials. As a result, even in circumstances where local jurisdictions have 
taken steps to make campaign disclosure reports broadly available by moving to 
an electronic reporting system, the local jurisdictions still must maintain paper 
versions of those reports." 
3 Senate Rules Committee, Senate Aoor Analysis of AB 2452 dated June 20, 2012. 


File No. 1-12-137 
Page No. 5 
Section 84615 sets forth requirements for local jurisdictions' electronic campaign filing 
programs. It is permissive, stating that a local jurisdiction may require candidates or committees 
(that raise or spend $1,000 or more per year) to file campaign reports electronically.4 The 
legislation further provides that local governments shall adopt an ordinance approving electronic 
filing which includes a legislative finding that the electronic filing system will operate securely 
and effectively and would not unduly burden filers.5 In addition, the local ordinance may, at the 
discretion of the legislative body, specify that the electronic filing requirements apply only to 
specifically identified types of filings or are triggered only by identified monetary thresholds. 
For example, a local jurisdiction's ordinance could specify that online or electronic filing applies 
only to committees that have raised or spent $5,000 or $10,000. The program must accept filings 
in the standardized record format developed by the Secretary of State, must ensure the integrity 
of the data transmitted, must include a procedure for filers to comply with the requirement that 
they sign statements and reports under penalty of perjury, and must generate a confirmation of 
filing. 
Thus, Section 84615 pennits cities and counties to mandate electronic filing of campaign 
reports for candidates and committees in their jurisdiction. However, Section 84615 does not 
prohibit a city or county from using a voluntary system of electronic filing as Marin County 
currently does, permitting candidates and committees who choose to file campaign reports 
electronically to do so, and permitting others who do not want to file electronically to file 
campaign reports on paper. Further, we interpret Section 84615(j) to mean that if a candidate or 
committee files campaign reports electronically with a local jurisdiction, they are not required to 
file a paper copy of that document with the local jurisdiction. Similarly, assuming the electronic 
filing system Marin County is using contains a procedure for filers to comply with the 
requirement that they sign statements and reports under penalty of perjury under Section 81004, 
as required by Section 84615(g) for electronically filed statements, you would not have to 
request that the original signature be separately filed on paper. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
HPW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
71-flA P. w {) 
Hyla P. Wagner 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
4 Mandatory electronic filing cannot be required for candidates and committees that raise or spend less than 
$ I ,000 per year. 
5 It does not appear that Marin's 2010 campaign ordinance includes a mandate and legislative finding 
related to electronic filing.

of your local filers to file electronically?

from continuing its policy of allowing candidates and committees to file campaign reports either 
electronically or on paper. Further. we interpret Section 84615(j) to mean that for those filings 
that a local jurisdiction does receive electronically. no paper copy is required.

.. A local government agency may require an elected officer, candidate, 
committee, or other person required to file statements. reports, or other documents 
required by Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 84100), except an elected

or other documents online or electronically with a local filing officer. A local

and effectively and would not unduly burden filers. The ordinance adopted by the

only to specifically identified types of filings or are triggered only by identified
